body.label: Body
body.description: How heavy the wine feels on your palate

wines.name: Vinho Verde
wines.hint: Atlantic Portugal, slight spritz, young vines
wines.reveal: Featherlight — ~9% ABV, no oak, high acid, slight spritz
wines.compare: Low alcohol and spritz give it far less weight than {next}.

wines.name: Muscadet
wines.hint: Loire Atlantic coast, neutral grape, sur lie tradition
wines.reveal: Bone-dry, lean, ocean-spray minerality — no oak
wines.compare: More mid-palate presence from sur lie aging, but still firmly light.

wines.name: Pinot Grigio
wines.hint: Northern Italian, stainless steel fermentation
wines.reveal: Light, crisp — cool ferment, early bottling, no oak
wines.compare: Slightly more fruit extract, but stainless steel keeps it light.

wines.name: Albarino
wines.hint: Galician coast, aromatic white, granite soils
wines.reveal: Aromatic, peach, saline — unoaked but more extract
wines.compare: More aromatic extract and slightly higher alcohol give it more presence.

wines.name: Chablis
wines.hint: Cool-climate Burgundy, Kimmeridgian limestone
wines.reveal: Steely, unoaked, citrus — but Chardonnay's natural extract adds weight
wines.compare: Chardonnay's inherent extract gives this more palate weight.

wines.name: White Burgundy
wines.hint: Cote de Beaune, barrel-influenced Chardonnay
wines.reveal: Nutty, mineral, partial oak — MLF adds creamy texture
wines.compare: Barrel fermentation and MLF give this a creamy richness.

wines.name: Viognier
wines.hint: Northern Rhone, aromatic white, steep granite slopes
wines.reveal: Floral, stone fruit, oily texture — naturally low acid, high extract
wines.compare: Viognier's naturally low acid and high glycerol make it feel heavier.

wines.name: Oaked Napa Chardonnay
wines.hint: Warm-climate California, new oak program
wines.reveal: Rich, buttery, vanilla — ~14% ABV, new oak, full MLF
wines.compare: High alcohol, new oak, and full MLF make this the heaviest.

acidity.label: Acidity
acidity.description: The sharpness and freshness that makes your mouth water

wines.name: Viognier
wines.hint: Northern Rhone, aromatic white, steep granite slopes
wines.reveal: Soft, oily texture — inherently one of the lowest-acid white grapes
wines.compare: Genetically one of the lowest-acid white grapes — noticeably softer.

wines.name: Gewurztraminer
wines.hint: Alsace, highly aromatic, pink-skinned grape
wines.reveal: Low acid despite cool climate — genetically a low-acid grape with oily mouthfeel
wines.compare: Low-acid grape, but Alsace's climate gives it a touch more freshness.

wines.name: Oaked Chardonnay
wines.hint: Warm-climate California, barrel fermented
wines.reveal: Soft, buttery — full MLF converts malic to softer lactic acid
wines.compare: MLF softens the acid, but Chardonnay naturally has more structure.

wines.name: Pinot Gris
wines.hint: Alsace, mutation of Pinot Noir, smoky character
wines.reveal: Medium acid — more than Gewurztraminer, less than Sauvignon Blanc
wines.compare: Medium acid — more freshness than the soft butteriness of oaked Chardonnay.

wines.name: Sauvignon Blanc
wines.hint: New Zealand South Island, cool maritime climate
wines.reveal: Zingy, grapefruit — naturally high-acid grape amplified by cool climate
wines.compare: High natural acid amplified by cool climate — notably sharper.

wines.name: Muscadet
wines.hint: Loire Atlantic coast, neutral grape, sur lie tradition
wines.reveal: Razor-sharp, lemony — neutral grape lets the bracing acid sing unmasked
wines.compare: The neutral grape lets the acid sing unmasked — sharper than aromatic fruit.

wines.name: Assyrtiko
wines.hint: Greek volcanic island, ancient bush vines
wines.reveal: Searing acid even in heat — volcanic soils amplify the electric character
wines.compare: Retains piercing acidity even in Santorini's heat — more electric.

wines.name: Champagne Brut
wines.hint: France's northernmost vineyards, traditional method
wines.reveal: Razor acid — underripe grapes + CO2 amplify perceived acidity to extremes
wines.compare: Underripe grapes + CO2 amplification = highest perceived acidity.

sweetness.label: Sweetness
sweetness.description: How much residual sugar remains after fermentation

wines.name: Muscadet
wines.hint: Loire Atlantic coast, neutral grape, sur lie tradition
wines.reveal: Bone-dry — virtually zero residual sugar (<2 g/L)
wines.compare: Fermented completely dry — less residual sugar than anything.

wines.name: Sancerre
wines.hint: Central Loire, Kimmeridgian and flint soils
wines.reveal: Dry, flinty — Loire Sauvignon is always vinified bone-dry
wines.compare: Also bone-dry, but fruit aromatics can give a slightly riper impression.

wines.name: Alsace Riesling
wines.hint: Alsace, continental climate, varied geology
wines.reveal: Typically dry, though some producers leave a whisper of RS
wines.compare: Typically dry, though less predictably so — some bottles are off-dry.

wines.name: Riesling Kabinett
wines.hint: Mosel steep slate slopes, Pradikat system
wines.reveal: Off-dry — 30-60 g/L RS, but extreme acidity masks the sweetness
wines.compare: The residual sugar is measurable (30-60 g/L) — a clear step up in sweetness.

wines.name: Vouvray Demi-Sec
wines.hint: Touraine, tuffeau limestone caves, Chenin Blanc
wines.reveal: Medium-sweet — demi-sec means ~20-40 g/L RS, perceptibly sweet
wines.compare: The demi-sec designation means noticeably sweeter.

wines.name: Moscato d'Asti
wines.hint: Piedmont hills, aromatic Muscat family, frizzante
wines.reveal: Sweet — fermentation stopped early to retain ~100 g/L RS at ~5.5% ABV
wines.compare: Stopped fermentation leaves far more sugar than demi-sec styles.

wines.name: Sauternes
wines.hint: Left Bank Bordeaux, morning fog, Semillon-based
wines.reveal: Luscious — noble rot concentrates sugars to 120-150 g/L RS
wines.compare: Botrytized grapes produce far more concentrated sweetness.

wines.name: Pedro Ximenez
wines.hint: Andalucia, solera system, sun-dried grape tradition
wines.reveal: Treacle-sweet — sun-dried grapes concentrate sugar to 300-400 g/L RS
wines.compare: Sun-dried grapes produce 300-400 g/L RS — roughly double Sauternes.

tannin.label: Tannin
tannin.description: The drying, gripping sensation from grape skins and oak

wines.name: Beaujolais
wines.hint: Granite hills south of Burgundy, carbonic maceration
wines.reveal: Juicy, silky — carbonic maceration extracts color but minimal tannin
wines.compare: Carbonic maceration and thin skins mean virtually zero grip.

wines.name: Pinot Noir
wines.hint: Cote d'Or limestone, whole-cluster tradition
wines.reveal: Delicate tannin — thin-skinned, gentle extraction, silky texture
wines.compare: Thin skins give delicate tannin, but more structure than carbonic juiciness.

wines.name: Grenache
wines.hint: Southern Rhone, galets roules, old bush vines
wines.reveal: Soft, round tannins — thin skins despite high alcohol
wines.compare: Thin skins keep tannin soft despite concentration.

wines.name: Merlot
wines.hint: Right Bank Bordeaux, clay-limestone plateau
wines.reveal: Plush, medium tannin — medium-thick skins, velvety rather than gripping
wines.compare: Medium tannin — plush rather than gripping, but more structured.

wines.name: Syrah
wines.hint: Steep granite slopes, continental-meets-Mediterranean
wines.reveal: Firm, fine-grained tannin — thick-skinned with clear structural grip
wines.compare: Thick skins and firm extraction — a clear step up in grip.

wines.name: Cabernet Sauvignon
wines.hint: Warm Napa benchland, volcanic and alluvial soils
wines.reveal: Firm, powerful tannin — very thick skins, high pip-to-juice ratio, new oak
wines.compare: Thick skins + high pip ratio + new oak = more structural grip.

wines.name: Tannat
wines.hint: Southwest France, Pyrenean foothills
wines.reveal: Massive tannin — the grape's name literally comes from tannin
wines.compare: Named for its tannin — more astringent and chewy.

wines.name: Barolo
wines.hint: Langhe hills, calcareous marl, long aging tradition
wines.reveal: Fierce tannin, pale color — long maceration produces decades of grip
wines.compare: Pale color belies extreme tannin — long maceration produces grip that rivals the highest.

alcohol.label: Alcohol
alcohol.description: The warmth and heat you feel, driven by climate and winemaking

wines.name: Moscato d'Asti
wines.hint: Piedmont hills, aromatic Muscat family, frizzante
wines.reveal: ~5.5% ABV — fermentation stopped very early to retain sweetness
wines.compare: Deliberately stopped fermentation keeps this at ~5.5%.

wines.name: Riesling Kabinett
wines.hint: Mosel steep slate slopes, Pradikat system
wines.reveal: ~8% ABV — cool climate + early harvest + residual sugar = low alcohol
wines.compare: At ~8%, notably higher, but still well below table wine levels.

wines.name: Vinho Verde
wines.hint: Atlantic Portugal, granite soils, pergola-trained vines
wines.reveal: ~9.5% ABV — Atlantic influence, picked relatively early
wines.compare: Early picking keeps alcohol moderate — higher but still light.

wines.name: Prosecco
wines.hint: Veneto hills, Charmat method, tank-fermented
wines.reveal: ~11% ABV — Charmat method and cool-climate Glera keep it moderate
wines.compare: The Charmat method keeps alcohol at ~11% — a step up.

wines.name: Sancerre
wines.hint: Central Loire, Kimmeridgian and flint soils
wines.reveal: ~12.5% ABV — cool continental climate, WSET's medium band
wines.compare: Cool climate keeps this at ~12.5%, a notable step up.

wines.name: Oaked Chardonnay
wines.hint: Warm-climate California, new oak program
wines.reveal: ~14.5% ABV — warm Napa climate fully ripens grapes, WSET's high band
wines.compare: Warm climate drives full ripeness — a big jump to the high band.

wines.name: Barossa Shiraz
wines.hint: South Australian warmth, old vine heritage
wines.reveal: ~15% ABV — hot, dry climate produces very ripe grapes
wines.compare: Hot climate and full extraction push this notably higher.

wines.name: Amarone
wines.hint: Veneto, appassimento tradition, dried-grape method
wines.reveal: ~15.5-16% ABV — grape drying concentrates sugar before fermentation
wines.compare: Dried grapes concentrate sugar beyond what any climate alone produces.
